summary: Gruber traces the evolution from Telepath, a civility-focused social network that never took off, to Wavelength, a group messaging app from the same team that he finds compelling enough to join as an official adviser. He argues Wavelength fills a gap between personal messaging apps (Messages, Signal, WhatsApp) optimized for one-on-one chats and enterprise platforms (Slack, Discord) built for massive organizations. The key design insight is a three-level hierarchy — groups, threads, messages — that makes large group chats manageable without the complexity of channels. Gruber also highlights Wavelength's integration of GPT-3.5 AI into group conversations as a genuinely novel feature, and its commitment to native iOS/Mac apps with end-to-end encryption. He discloses his advisory role and equity stake while making the case that Wavelength deserves attention despite its chicken-and-egg adoption challenge.
key_points: Wavelength occupies a neglected middle ground between personal messaging apps (optimized for 1-on-1) and enterprise platforms like Slack (optimized for thousands), targeting groups that could fill a bus or airplane.
The three-level hierarchy of groups → threads → messages is a deceptively simple design innovation that makes large group chats feel manageable rather than overwhelming.
Gruber advised the team to replace their clever but impractical thread-card UI with a high-density list view inspired by Apple Mail, which led to his formal advisory role.
AI chat integrated into group conversations is a fundamentally different and more interesting experience than one-on-one AI chat, and no other messaging platform offers this yet.
Wavelength is built as a native iOS/Mac app with state-of-the-art end-to-end encryption, including a novel peer-to-peer history sync feature unique among encrypted messengers.
money_quotes: One of my strongest product design beliefs is that it matters, greatly, where you start conceptually. Every successful software platform evolves, but its origin, the core, pervades forever.
The difference made by adding threads as an additional hierarchical layer is so simple to understand that it feels obvious, not designed per se but merely discovered. But the difference is profound.
Presentation-wise, this thread view was the most original thing in Wavelength, and I felt certain they ought to throw it out.
Wavelength is the opposite of cacophonous. It's visually quiet. It looks a lot like what I'd imagine a new 'Messages for Groups' app from Apple itself would look like.
Joining a Wavelength group is no heavier a task nor any more of a commitment than, say, following an RSS feed. Don't like a group? Just leave.
Group chat with an AI bot as a member is both fun and useful.
You either get why native apps are essential, experience-wise, or you don't. The Wavelength team gets it.
It's 'AI with friends' — and no other group messaging platform has this yet.
key_insight: The best group messaging experience comes not from adding features to personal chat apps or simplifying enterprise platforms, but from starting with a group-first design built around a simple three-level hierarchy of groups, threads, and messages.
